policy_statement: Enact policies that support electrified renovations and new construction by 2023.
mechanism_description: Support for electrified renovations and new buildings shifts end-uses from fossil fuels to electricity, reducing direct on-site fossil fuel combustion and emissions.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Electrification (especially via heat pumps and efficient electric appliances) often delivers the same heating/service with less final energy input than combustion systems, reducing overall energy demand in addition to cutting direct fuel use.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy service provision → electrified end-uses replace on-site combustion → ↓ direct fossil fuel consumption → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Building renovations and new construction adopt electric HVAC/water heating/cooking (often heat pumps) instead of gas/oil/propane equipment.  
(2) Process: Reduced on-site combustion eliminates point-of-use CO₂ (and methane leakage/combustion-related emissions), shifting energy supply to the electricity system.  
(3) Outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions (immediate from avoided direct combustion; larger over time as electricity generation decarbonizes), with potential co-benefits like improved indoor air quality.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from avoiding direct fossil fuel combustion in buildings by switching end-uses to electricity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
(1) Dominant causal pathway: Switching building end-uses from fossil fuels to electricity reduces direct combustion emissions—this is a mitigation pathway.  
(2) Why this primary over alternatives: While electrification can improve comfort/resilience, the mechanism described and most universal effect is emissions reduction from reduced fossil fuel use.  
(3) Inherent secondary: Resource Efficiency is commonly inherent because many electrified technologies (notably heat pumps) provide the same service with lower energy input than combustion.  
(4) Edge cases applied: If the grid is very carbon-intensive, near-term total emissions benefits can be smaller or delayed, but the mechanism still targets direct fossil fuel reduction and enables future decarbonization.  
(5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy using this mechanism necessarily reduces on-site fossil fuel combustion; thus Mitigation applies broadly, and efficiency improvements are typically coupled to electrification technologies.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If electrification is implemented with resistance heating (not heat pumps) or in regions with very high-carbon electricity, the “Resource Efficiency” secondary may be weaker, but the primary mitigation classification (direct fossil fuel reduction) still holds.
co_benefits: Improved indoor air quality; potential reduced exposure to indoor combustion pollutants
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument type is ambiguous because “support” could imply incentives, codes, or mandates; classified as target-setting because the only concrete element is the time-bound commitment (“by 2023”) without specifying the policy tool.
